steps.title: Margin of error creates a confidence interval
steps.content: When a survey reports a sample estimate with a margin of error, it defines a range of plausible values for the true population parameter. The margin of error is applied symmetrically: we go both up and down from the estimate.

steps.title: Calculate the confidence interval
steps.content:
With a sample estimate of 38% and a margin of error of 5.5%, the plausible range is:
$$38\% - 5.5\% = 32.5\%\quad\text{and}\quad 38\% + 5.5\% = 43.5\%$$
The interval is [32.5%, 43.5%].

steps.title: Identify the correct interpretation
steps.content:
Checking each choice:
  • A claims the true value is exactly 38% — too definitive; the true value could anywhere in the margin of error.
  • B claims the true value is greater than 38% — not guaranteed; it could be 32.5% or lower.
  • C states plausible values are between 32.5% and 43.5% — this is precisely what the margin of error establishes.
  • D refers to numbers of students (295–305) rather than percentages, and those values do not correspond to the data (38% of 300 = 114 students, not near 300).
That matches choice C — C is correct.
